title: Post-Event Chargeback After Verified Digital Ticket Redemption and Gate Scan
narrative: Cardholder purchases premium event tickets ($850-$2,400 range) using verified digital payment, receives QR-code tickets via email within 2-3 minutes, successfully redeems tickets at venue entry (scanned 24-72 hours post-purchase), attends full event, then files chargeback 15-45 days later claiming non-receipt or unauthorized transaction. Pattern clusters around high-demand concerts, sports finals, and theater productions. Transactions show legitimate P_emaildomain match to cardholder, successful AVS/CVV checks, and normal device_id consistency. Dispute filed exactly 1-2 days after event conclusion when merchant can no longer reverse digital delivery. Cardholder often files as 'merchandise not received' despite ProductCD = 'W' (digital goods) and documented redemption timestamp in merchant system. Repeat offenders show 2-4 similar disputes across different ticketing MIDs within 6-month window, always post-redemption.
indicators: TransactionAmt $850-$2,400 for ProductCD = 'W' with event-ticketing MCC 7922 or 7941
Chargeback filed 15-45 days post-transaction, specifically 1-2 days after scheduled event date
P_emaildomain matches known consumer domains (gmail.com, yahoo.com, outlook.com) with 90+ day account age
card1_chargeback_count_180d shows 2-4 disputes all coded 'non-receipt' for digital/event purchases
Transaction shows AVS = 'Y', cvv_match = 1, dist1 < 50km (local cardholder to billing)
Merchant metadata confirms QR redemption timestamp 24-72 hours post-purchase
card1_txn_count_7d = 1-2 (isolated high-value purchase, not velocity pattern)
summary: Customer buys expensive digital event tickets, successfully scans them at the venue gate and attends the event, then files a chargeback 1-2 days after the event claiming they never received the tickets or the charge was unauthorized.
reasoning: The cardholder is deliberately exploiting the chargeback system to get a refund after consuming the service—they attended the event they paid for, then falsely disputed the charge to keep both the experience and their money.
indicator_explanations: Large spend on digital event tickets at entertainment or concert venues
Dispute arrives suspiciously soon after the event already happened
Legitimate personal email with established history, not throwaway account
Repeat pattern of claiming non-receipt on digital goods they likely received
Perfect payment verification and cardholder is local to billing address
Evidence shows customer actually scanned and used the tickets at event
Single big-ticket purchase without suspicious transaction velocity or pattern
